content: Just as language learners use deliberate written reflection (taught in high English language arts curricula) to refine implicit writing intuition and catch unrecognized logical gaps, large language models can integrate graded, curriculum-style self-reflection routines to iteratively polish their output, rather than relying on one-off contrastive self-reflection checks. This structured, sequential reflection framework borrowed from second-language learning addresses a key limitation of current LLM self-reflection, which often only compares inconsistent solutions after generation instead of building consistent reflective habits over multiple attempts.
